records.ety: [OE. heven, hebben, AS. hebban; akin to OS. hebbian, D. heffen, OHG. heffan, hevan, G. heben, Icel. hefja, Sw. häfva, Dan. hæve, Goth. hafjan, L. capere to take, seize; cf. Gr. kw`ph handle. Cf. Accept, Behoof, Capacious, Forceps, Haft, Receipt.]
records.morph: [imp. Heaved (hēvd), or Hove (hōv); p. p. Heaved, Hove, formerly Hoven (hō"v'n); p. pr. & vb. n. Heaving.]

defs: To cause to move upward or onward by a lifting effort; to lift; to raise; to hoist; -- often with up; as, the wave heaved the boat on land.
quotes: One heaved ahigh, to be hurled down below.
Here a little child I stand, Heaving up my either hand.
notes: ☞ Heave, as now used, implies that the thing raised is heavy or hard to move; but formerly it was used in a less restricted sense.

defs: To throw; to cast; -- obsolete, provincial, or colloquial, except in certain nautical phrases; as, to heave the lead; to heave the log.
To force from, or into, any position; to cause to move; also, to throw off; -- mostly used in certain nautical phrases; as, to heave the ship ahead.
To raise or force from the breast; to utter with effort; as, to heave a sigh.
quotes: The wretched animal heaved forth such groans.

defs: To cause to swell or rise, as the breast or bosom.
quotes: The glittering, finny swarms That heave our friths, and crowd upon our shores.

defs: To be thrown up or raised; to rise upward, as a tower or mound.
quotes: And the huge columns heave into the sky.
Where heaves the turf in many a moldering heap.
The heaving sods of Bunker Hill.

defs: To rise and fall with alternate motions, as the lungs in heavy breathing, as waves in a heavy sea, as ships on the billows, as the earth when broken up by frost, etc.; to swell; to dilate; to expand; to distend; hence, to labor; to struggle.
quotes: Frequent for breath his panting bosom heaves.

defs: To make an effort to raise, throw, or move anything; to strain to do something difficult.
quotes: The Church of England had struggled and heaved at a reformation ever since Wyclif's days.

defs: To make an effort to vomit; to retch; to vomit.
An effort to raise something, as a weight, or one's self, or to move something heavy.
quotes: After many strains and heaves He got up to his saddle eaves.

defs: An upward motion; a rising; a swell or distention, as of the breast in difficult breathing, of the waves, of the earth in an earthquake, and the like.
quotes: There's matter in these sighs, these profound heaves, You must translate.
None could guess whether the next heave of the earthquake would settle . . . or swallow them.

defs: A horizontal dislocation in a metallic lode, taking place at an intersection with another lode.
